Original Persona: A permaculture enthusiast who invites the teacher to visit their sustainable garden for inspiration and hands-on learning
Expanded Persona:
Maya Carter is a transgender female (they/them), African American permaculture enthusiast born in 1987. They're in their late 30s and transforms small urban plots into dense, edible, low-water gardens. They invite the teacher to visit their sustainable garden for inspiration and hands-on learning, offering practical demonstrations on soil building, companion planting, and rainwater capture. They enjoy mentoring students, sharing accessible techniques, and collaborating on curriculum projects that tie ecology to social justice. Comfortable teaching outdoors, they keep projects low-cost and community-focused, blending traditional knowledge with modern permaculture design.

01/25/2006.Event: Began formal study of permaculture principles and committed to designing low-water, edible garden systems for urban spaces; this formed the backbone of their approach for years.

06/15/2007.Event: Every morning collects kitchen scraps and yard trimmings for compost, inspects soil structure, and adjusts beds — a daily routine that supports soil-building practices.

09/10/2008.Event: Started mentoring local students and educators, co-developing curriculum that links ecology to social justice and emphasizing accessible techniques for community use over multiple years.

04/05/2009.Event: Every weekend leads hands-on demonstrations in companion planting and bed preparation for neighbors and students, preferring practical, outdoor instruction to lecture formats.

11/20/2009.Event: Ran into a major setback when a larger development threatened a community garden site they had helped establish; responded by redesigning small, dense plots and organizing community advocacy to protect urban growing space.

03/03/2010.Event: Today chooses to teach outdoors whenever possible and avoids indoor-only classrooms because they find field demonstrations more effective for learning and engagement.

07/18/2011.Event: Maintains a long-term practice of transforming small urban plots into dense, edible gardens that prioritize low water use and high yield, partnering with neighbors to keep projects low-cost and community-centered.

12/01/2012.Event: Today refuses to use chemical pesticides or indiscriminate herbicides in garden work, documenting pest management through companion planting and cultural controls as a routine choice.

05/14/2014.Event: Over several years integrated traditional ecological knowledge with modern permaculture design in lesson plans and site plans, emphasizing rainwater capture, companion planting, and low-cost materials.

07/20/2008.[Reasons of Change]: After months of rapid mutual‑aid runs, Maya developed a hybrid minimalist kit using modular protective inserts that preserved heirloom materials while dramatically improving mobility — a pragmatic, uncommon fastening of minimalist principles to community logistics.
07/20/2008.Event: Assembled and field‑tested a modular minimalist kit with waterproof inserts to carry seeds, hand tools, and printed curricula across multiple rapid-stop outreach days.

08/15/2008.[Old Event]: Suspended public volunteer route-testing after repeated safety incidents during a transit strike and began compiling and annotating official transit data for community distribution instead.
08/15/2008.[Reasons of Change]: A transit authority appointed a community liaison and funded safety escorts for volunteer auditors after listening sessions; with these structural protections in place, Maya resumed in-field testing because it once again provided essential, ground-truth access information for mobility-limited neighbors.
08/15/2008.Event: Relaunched volunteer transit-route testing under a formal safety-escort agreement and published updated annotated maps for accessible planning.

09/01/2008.Event: Published an updated community trip-budget template that automates sliding-scale calculations and includes a short FAQ for hosts and visiting participants on expected expenses.

09/20/2008.[Old Event]: Committed to reintroducing at least one weeklong slow-immersion visit per program cycle to preserve depth of learning and participant wellbeing.
09/20/2008.[Reasons of Change]: A rapid increase in urgent, short-term requests from multiple community partners and a time-limited donor challenge to expand reach forced Maya to favor frequent, shorter micro-visits to more neighborhoods rather than fewer deep immersions — an operational shift driven by rare external demand and time-limited funding.
09/20/2008.Event: Adopted a program model emphasizing shorter, frequent site visits across several neighborhoods instead of multiple weeklong immersions for a twelve-month campaign.

10/05/2008.[Old Event]: Paused the public blog and switched to a closed, consent-based newsletter for supporters to avoid press-driven exposure of host families.
10/05/2008.[Reasons of Change]: A targeted, anonymized storytelling campaign with strict consent and a subscriber paywall successfully unlocked matching funds for land protection; the rare, ethically-managed public narrative proved powerful enough to justify a carefully controlled return to selective blogging.
10/05/2008.Event: Restarted a consent-first, subscriber-access travel blog that publishes anonymized case studies to support a land-protection fundraising stream.

11/11/2008.[Old Event]: Experienced a lengthy, unpredictable visa process for a multi-country workshop and concluded they will avoid itineraries requiring complex visas unless absolutely necessary.
11/11/2008.[Reasons of Change]: A student volunteer faced imminent deportation due to expired paperwork after an international placement; Maya assembled a pro bono visa-assistance toolkit and negotiated a local legal clinic partnership to prevent future harm — an uncommon, emergency-driven turn from avoidance to active engagement with visa systems.
11/11/2008.Event: Co-created a community visa-assistance toolkit and partnered with a legal clinic to help low-income participants navigate short-term travel permissions.

12/01/2008.[Old Event]: Committed to a multi-year nature-trail scouting project to identify and document low-impact walking routes near urban neighborhoods for accessible day trips.
12/01/2008.[Reasons of Change]: Repeated observations of off-leash dog disturbances and newly proposed recreational development threatening nesting sites led Maya to conclude that casual scouting was causing inadvertent harm; to protect sensitive habitat, they shifted from scouting to formal habitat-advocacy and permit review — an uncommon pivot from recreation to conservation bureaucracy.
12/01/2008.Event: Paused recreational trail scouting and filed formal habitat-protection requests with the park authority while training volunteers in low-impact monitoring protocols.
